title: Studying in Australia
description: If you come to Australia to study, check what you need to consider if you start working.
keywords: australian assets, australian property, australian resident, foreign resident, non-resident, temporary resident, australian sourced income, australian worker, backpacker, certificate of payment, foreign employment income, foreign income, foreign investments, foreign property, foreign resident, foreign salary, foreign worker, gap year, going overseas, holiday visa, international students, international visitor, investing overseas, work in Australia, working holiday

content_blocks.text: If you're an overseas student and enrolled to study in Australia in a course that lasts for 6 months or more, you may be an Australian resident for tax purposes. Generally, if you are an Australian resident you need to declare all income you've earned both in Australia and overseas on your Australian tax return. This means:

items: you pay tax on your earnings at the same rate as other residents

you're entitled to the benefits of the Australian tax system, such as the tax-free threshold (or part of it, if you're here for only part of the financial year) tax offsets generally lower tax rates than a foreign resident.

content_blocks.text: As an overseas student you probably have a temporary visa External Link , which means that you may be a temporary resident as well as an Australian resident or foreign resident. For more information, see foreign and temporary resident income .

If you're a temporary resident and an Australian resident, most of your foreign income is not taxed in Australia and you don't declare it on your Australian tax return. You only declare income you derive in Australia, plus some income you earn from employment or services performed overseas while you are a temporary resident of Australia.
To read examples about residency, see Australian and foreign resident examples .
For more information about what to do when leaving Australia after working in Australia, see Returning to your home country .

content_text:
## Overseas students
If you're an overseas student and enrolled to study in Australia in a course that lasts for 6 months or more, you may be an Australian resident for tax purposes. Generally, if you are an Australian resident you need to declare all income you've earned both in Australia and overseas on your Australian tax return. This means:
- you pay tax on your earnings at the same rate as other residents
- you're entitled to the benefits of the Australian tax system, such as the tax-free threshold (or part of it, if you're here for only part of the financial year) tax offsets generally lower tax rates than a foreign resident.
## Temporary residents
As an overseas student you probably have a temporary visa External Link , which means that you may be a temporary resident as well as an Australian resident or foreign resident. For more information, see foreign and temporary resident income .
If you're a temporary resident and an Australian resident, most of your foreign income is not taxed in Australia and you don't declare it on your Australian tax return. You only declare income you derive in Australia, plus some income you earn from employment or services performed overseas while you are a temporary resident of Australia.
To read examples about residency, see Australian and foreign resident examples .
For more information about what to do when leaving Australia after working in Australia, see Returning to your home country .
